summary: Dan Luu optimizes his Octopress static blog hosted on GitHub Pages, achieving a 25-50x speedup by removing unnecessary JavaScript, fonts, and CSS. Starting with a 12-second load time, he systematically eliminates bloat like unused modernizr/jQuery dependencies, custom fonts, and unminified CSS to reach 400ms. The post demonstrates how someone with zero web development knowledge can achieve GPU-to-ASIC-level performance gains simply by removing the default cruft that comes with popular frameworks, raising questions about why widely-used platforms ship with such massive performance problems.
key_points: Moved JavaScript from head to footer, eliminating 4-second render-blocking delay
Removed unused Octopress.js features (flash videos, delicious integration) and all JavaScript since the only feature was an unused sidebar toggle
Eliminated custom web fonts which comprised 43.8% of page payload despite images being negligible
Minified and inlined CSS, removing support for obsolete browsers and unused features, cutting CSS by half
Switched to Cloudflare with 'Cache Everything' page rule since GitHub Pages doesn't cache HTML for bare domains without DNS alias records
money_quotes: How is it possible that someone with zero knowledge of web development can get that kind of speedup by watching one presentation and then futzing around for 25 minutes?
I associate speedups of that magnitude with porting plain Ruby code to optimized C++, optimized C++ to a GPU, or GPU to quick-and-dirty exploratory ASIC.
Despite my reference post being an unusually image heavy blog post, fonts are 43.8% and images are such a small percentage that webpagetest doesn't even list the number.

I haven't seen a study that looks at going from 400ms to 10900ms because the idea that a website would be that slow is so absurd that people don't even look into the possibility. But many websites are that slow!

Having no styling is surely not optimal, but there's something a bit funny about no styling beating the at-the-time most widely used programmer blog styling, which means it likely also beat wordpress, svtble, blogspot, medium, etc.
key_insight: Popular web frameworks and platforms ship with such egregious performance problems that removing all their default features can yield hardware-optimization-level speedups, suggesting the industry has normalized absurdly slow websites.
